**Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research** — Nye, Su, Rounds, & Drasgow, 2012  
Vocational interests predict job performance, training performance, and persistence, particularly when people’s interests match their work environment. This suggests interesting, well-matched work can affect both happiness and career success.

**Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory** — Hackman & Oldham, 1976  
Work that provides autonomy, task significance, skill variety, task identity, and feedback tends to produce higher internal motivation and satisfaction. The paper implies that “interesting” work matters partly because it often bundles autonomy, challenge, and visible progress.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** — Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson, 2007  
A large meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, social support, and work context all predict job attitudes and performance. This supports prioritizing work design, but not focusing almost exclusively on intellectual interest.

**Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation** — Gagné & Deci, 2005  
Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central to high-quality motivation, well-being, and sustained performance at work. A leadership role could be attractive if it increases these needs, even if the technical work is less intrinsically interesting.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis** — Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford, 2014  
Intrinsic motivation predicts performance strongly, especially quality of work, while extrinsic incentives also predict performance, especially quantity. The evidence favors treating interesting work as very important, not as the only factor.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** — Deci, Koestner, & Ryan, 1999  
Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when they feel controlling. This is a warning against letting compensation pull you into work that feels less autonomous or less intrinsically engaging.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich, 2010  
Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction, and pay satisfaction depends heavily on fairness and comparison. For someone already highly compensated, a small salary increase is unlikely to compensate for a large drop in day-to-day work quality.

**Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved** — Killingsworth, Kahneman, & Mellers, 2023  
Well-being generally continues to rise with income, though the gains are smaller at higher income levels and some less-happy people show a plateau. This argues against ignoring salary entirely, but also against assuming a modest raise should dominate role quality.

**Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis** — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay, 2019  
Meaningful work is associated with higher job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and organizational commitment, and with lower withdrawal intentions. This supports prioritizing work that feels worthwhile, but meaning is broader than intellectual interestingness alone.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson, 2005

**Decision and Experience: Why Don’t We Choose What Makes Us Happy?** — Hsee & Hastie, 2006

- **Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
  Interesting, varied, autonomous, meaningful work with clear feedback is strongly linked to internal motivation and job satisfaction. This supports heavily weighting day-to-day work content, but not treating “interesting” as the only dimension.

- **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
  This meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, social support, and work context all predict satisfaction and performance. It supports prioritizing rich work, but also says team, manager, and role design matter.

- **Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis**  
  Intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially quality and complex work performance. Extrinsic incentives also matter, so the evidence does not support focusing on interestingness almost exclusively.

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
  External rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation when they feel controlling, especially for already-interesting tasks. This suggests salary and title should not be allowed to crowd out autonomy, curiosity, and ownership.

- **Immediate Rewards Predict Adherence to Long-Term Goals**  
  Immediate enjoyment predicts persistence on long-term goals better than abstract importance or delayed benefits. This is one of the stronger arguments that people underweight how enjoyable the daily work will feel.

- **For the Fun of It: Harnessing Immediate Rewards to Increase Persistence in Long-Term Goals**  
  Making goal pursuit enjoyable increases persistence across domains. For a job choice, this implies that “the work is interesting every day” may be more practically important than people expect.

- **Are You Interested? A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Vocational Interests and Employee Performance and Turnover**  
  Fit between a person’s interests and the job predicts performance and lower turnover. The effects are meaningful but not overwhelming, so interest should be central but not the sole criterion.

- **Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research**  
  Vocational interests predict academic, training, and job performance, especially when interests align with the environment. This supports staying close to work you genuinely like, particularly in research-heavy roles.

- **Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit**  
  Person–job and person–organization fit strongly predict satisfaction, commitment, and turnover intentions. “Interesting technical work” is only one part of fit; manager, team, culture, and role identity also count.

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
  Pay has a positive but relatively small relationship with job satisfaction. A modest salary increase is therefore unlikely to compensate fully for a large drop in daily interest.

- **Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved**  
  Higher income is associated with higher well-being for many people, but with diminishing and heterogeneous effects. This means money is not irrelevant, but at already-high compensation levels it may have less marginal value than work quality.

- **Predictors of Objective and Subjective Career Success: A Meta-Analysis**  
  Salary and promotions predict objective career success, while satisfaction depends on a broader mix of fit, support, personality, and career experiences. A leadership opportunity may matter if managing people is a core career goal, even if the technical content is less exciting.

- **Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work**  
  People who experience work as a calling report greater work and life satisfaction. The source of that calling can be intellectual interest, impact, mentorship, leadership, or identity—not just technical novelty.

- **Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?**  
  People who believe interests are fixed are more likely to disengage when a field becomes difficult or less immediately exciting. This cautions against treating current interestingness as perfectly stable or sufficient by itself.

**On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives — Heath (1999)**  
People tend to overestimate how much extrinsic incentives like pay motivate behavior and underestimate intrinsic motives like interest. This is direct evidence that interesting work can be underrated, though it does not imply salary and status are irrelevant.

**Immediate Rewards Predict Adherence to Long-Term Goals — Woolley & Fishbach (2017)**  
Immediate enjoyment predicts persistence toward long-term goals better than delayed rewards alone. For career choice, this suggests day-to-day interest in the work may matter more than people expect when comparing abstract benefits like title or compensation.

**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being — Ryan & Deci (2000)**  
Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central predictors of intrinsic motivation and well-being. A less interesting role may hurt if it reduces autonomy or competence, while a leadership role may help if it meaningfully increases autonomy and relatedness.

**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory — Hackman & Oldham (1976)**  
Skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback drive internal work motivation. This makes “interesting work” a core job-design variable rather than a superficial preference.

**The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis — Fried & Ferris (1987)**  
Job characteristics such as autonomy, variety, and feedback are consistently related to job satisfaction, motivation, and performance. The evidence supports heavily weighting work content, but not treating it as the only variable.

**Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do — Amabile (1997)**  
Intrinsic motivation is especially important for creativity and innovative work. For research engineering, a drop in intellectual interest could have disproportionate effects on both enjoyment and output.

**Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012)**  
People perform better when their work aligns with their vocational interests. Interest is therefore not just about happiness; it can also affect long-term career capital and achievement.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)**  
Person-job and person-organization fit predict job satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover. This supports comparing the whole fit of the two roles, not just compensation or title.

**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997)**  
People who experience their work as a calling report higher job and life satisfaction. If your current research feels close to a calling, that is a serious empirical consideration.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)**  
Pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest. This argues against ignoring salary, but also against expecting a modest salary increase to compensate for a major loss of interest.

**High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being — Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**  
Higher income improves life evaluation, but daily emotional well-being shows diminishing returns at higher income levels in their data. For already high earners, the quality of everyday work may matter more than incremental compensation.

**Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023)**  
Experienced well-being generally rises with log income for most people, though not equally for everyone. This weakens any simple “money stops mattering” claim, but still suggests money should not dominate if the work itself becomes much worse.

**People Who Choose Time Over Money Are Happier — Hershfield, Mogilner & Barnea (2016)**  
People who prioritize time over money report greater happiness. This supports valuing the lived experience of your workweek over a purely financial improvement.

**Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction — Schkade & Kahneman (1998)**  
People overestimate the importance of salient comparison features when predicting happiness. In an offer comparison, salary and title may loom larger than they will in daily life.

**Distinction Bias: Misprediction and Mischoice Due to Joint Evaluation — Hsee & Zhang (2004)**  
Side-by-side comparisons make quantitative differences seem more important than they feel in actual experience. A higher salary or formal team-lead title may be overweighted if the everyday work is substantially less engaging.

**The Value of Bosses — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton (2015)**  
Bosses have large measurable effects on worker productivity, showing that management can create real leverage. This supports taking the leadership opportunity seriously, but it does not show that leading less-interesting work will maximize your own well-being.
